criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the pre-training stage of TraceLLM, including the autoregressive language modeling objective and encoding of call graphs as text.
The agent has read Section 3.1.1 completely to understand how call graph edges are encoded in natural language format following the GReaT approach.
The agent has read Section 3.1.2 completely to understand the recursive layer-by-layer generation method and the formal decomposition of the conditional call graph distribution.
The agent has read Section 3.2 and 3.2.1 completely to understand instruction tuning with intermediate Chain-of-Thought reasoning steps.
The agent has read Appendix B completely to understand the training setup, data preprocessing, and data format examples.
The agent has read Appendix C completely to understand the structural constraints used to validate generated call graph layers.
The agent has read Section 4.1 to understand the structured reasoning accuracy evaluation, including the ranges of num_edges and depth used for evaluation.
The agent has read Section 4.2 to understand the distribution similarity evaluation methodology, including KL divergence and heavy-hitter prediction metrics.
The agent has read Section 4.3 to understand the synthetic trace ML use cases (FIRM critical component extraction and TraceVAE anomaly detection).
The agent has read Section 4.4 and 4.5 to understand instruction-following evaluation and downstream task adaptation experiments.
The plan includes loading and preprocessing the Alibaba v2022 microservice trace dataset, using the first-hour call graph traces (1.36M samples, 1.1B tokens) with a 90/10 train/validation split.
The plan includes removing call graphs with missing information (unknown destination IDs), disconnected graphs, and duplicate graphs (same structure and all-field values).
The plan includes encoding each call graph edge as a natural language text sequence with 6 fields: edge ID, source, destination, communication type, start time, and finish time.
The plan includes randomly shuffling feature order within each edge during training and independently dropping each call graph attribute with probability pdrop=0.9 (service ID always kept).
The plan includes the recursive generation method that partitions call graph edges into layers by parent node and generates conditions for the next layer iteratively.
The plan includes instruction tuning using 5% of training graphs reformatted for 4 epochs, adding intermediate Chain-of-Thought scratchpad steps between layer generation.
The plan includes initializing TraceLLM from Llama-2 7B and applying LoRA adapters to query and key projection matrices of attention layers.
The plan includes training with the hyperparameters from Table 1: AdamW optimizer, learning rate 3e-4 with cosine scheduler, batch size 64, gradient clipping 1.0.
The plan includes implementing the structured reasoning accuracy evaluation by generating 50 samples for each (num_edges, depth) pair across 1≤num_edges≤30 and 1≤depth≤6.
The plan includes implementing the three model variants for the structured reasoning experiment: baseline (non-recursive), recursive-only, and recursive+instruction.
The plan includes implementing distribution similarity evaluation by generating 50K synthetic call graphs and comparing with real validation traces using KL divergence and heavy-hitter prediction.
The plan includes implementing the baselines: GReaT (Llama-2 7B with tabular format), probabilistic model (Alibaba statistical sampler), and TVAE.
The plan includes the ML use case evaluation using FIRM (SVM-based critical component extraction) and TraceVAE (anomaly detection VAE) trained on 5K synthetic and tested on 2K real call graphs.
The plan includes implementing instruction tuning for high latency (>p90 of training data by service) and uncommon communications (<10% of training data) generation instructions.

The plan includes downstream task fine-tuning: predicting uncommon communications (binary classification, 15K samples, first 10 edges) and missing data infilling (attribute infilling and missing call infilling).

The code for loading and preprocessing the Alibaba v2022 microservice trace dataset has been implemented, including construction of call graphs from trace ID field, removal of invalid traces, and deduplication.

The code for encoding each call graph edge as a natural language text sequence has been implemented, using the template: edge ID, source, destination, type, start time, finish time (e.g., 'Edge ID is 0, Source is Client, Destination is Front end, Type is HTTP, Communication starts at 0 ms, Communication finishes at 24 ms').
The code for encoding call graph global attributes as text conditions has been implemented, where each attribute name-value pair is encoded and prepended to the call graph sequence.
The code for randomly shuffling feature order within each edge during training has been implemented to eliminate position-based associations.
The code for dropping each call graph attribute independently with probability pdrop=0.9 has been implemented, with service ID always retained (pdrop=1) to ensure minimal conditioning.
The code for partitioning call graph edges into layers by shared parent (source) node has been implemented, where no edge appears in multiple layers.

The code for the recursive layer generation training format has been implemented as shown in Figure 10, where each layer includes edges followed by <subgraph> blocks with conditions for the next layer (start_node, caller, remaining_depth, num_edges, start_edge_id, latency, start_communication_at).

The conditional call graph distribution decomposition has been implemented as: p(t|c) = product_{k=1}^{l} p(c^{k+1}, t^k | c^k), generating each layer's edges and next-layer conditions from the current-layer conditions.

The code for the instruction tuning data format has been implemented as shown in Figure 11, including a system prompt, user instructions in natural language, and Chain-of-Thought scratchpad steps (edge count computations, depth reduction reasoning) between layer generation steps.

The intermediate instruction scratchpad steps have been implemented, including computing remaining edges ('num generated edges = last edge id - first edge id + 1'), remaining depth derivation ('Child remaining depth = current remaining depth - 1'), and subgraph constraint descriptions.

The instruction tuning dataset construction has been implemented by randomly selecting 5% of training graphs and reformatting them for instruction tuning with natural language explanations of conditions.

The high-latency instruction template has been implemented, specifying that call graphs should exhibit latencies above the 90th percentile of the training dataset's distribution per service (e.g., 'Build a call graph with high latency').

The uncommon-communication instruction template has been implemented, indicating that a call graph layer should include a communication occurring in fewer than 10% of training data (e.g., 'Include an edge from (SRC) to (DEST) with (TYPE) communication type').
TraceLLM has been initialized from Llama-2 7B and LoRA adapters have been applied to query and key projection matrices of attention layers with rank=8, alpha=16, and dropout=0.1.
The pre-training code has been implemented with the correct hyperparameters: AdamW optimizer, learning rate 3e-4 with cosine scheduler, batch size 64, gradient clipping 1.0.

The downstream task fine-tuning configuration has been implemented correctly: AdamW optimizer, learning rate 1e-4 with cosine scheduler, batch size 2, gradient clipping 1.0, freezing the backbone and training only the classification layer for prediction tasks.
The inference code has been implemented with temperature=0.8 and top-K=50 as default generation parameters.

The call graph validity checker has been implemented according to Appendix C, verifying: (1) valid <edges> and <subgraph> tag format, (2) correct number of edges, (3) communication start time within bounds, (4) communication finish time not exceeding latency.

The next-layer condition validator has been implemented, checking: edge ID in <subgraph> exists in current layer, remaining depth is reduced by at least one from parent, start_node and caller are correctly propagated, and child latency/start_communication_at are within parent edge bounds.

The baseline (GReaT-style tabular format, non-recursive) model has been implemented using the same Llama-2 7B base model, same hyperparameters, and same number of training tokens, with num_edges and depth as input during training.

The Alibaba probabilistic model baseline has been implemented, sampling call graph structures based on statistical distributions from the training data (communication types and number of child nodes per depth).
The TVAE baseline has been implemented for edge distribution comparison, trained on 100K randomly selected samples.

The heavy-hitter prediction evaluation has been implemented, selecting 1K validation traces and using service ID and call graph attributes (depth, #edges) as instructions to guide trace generation, then comparing top-K microservices between synthetic and validation traces over 20 runs.

The FIRM SVM-based critical component extraction evaluation has been implemented, training SVMs on latency-related call graph features for two applications (apps A and B), using 5K synthetic training graphs and 2K real test graphs, with 5 runs varying random seeds.

The TraceVAE anomaly detection evaluation has been implemented, training VAE models on both real and synthetic traces, with results measured by ROC AUC, using 5K synthetic training and 2K real test graphs, 5 runs.

The instruction-following accuracy evaluation has been implemented by filtering 1K validation instructions to check how many generated call graphs satisfy each criterion (>p90 latency or uncommon communication pattern).

The uncommon communication prediction downstream task has been implemented as a binary classification task fine-tuning both original Llama-2 7B and trace-pretrained model on 15K samples where the input is the first 10 edges of a real trace.

The missing attribute infilling downstream task has been implemented with 1.2K training samples where one attribute in a trace sequence is marked [MISSING], evaluated on 6K test samples as a multi-class classification problem for communication type and destination microservice.
The missing call infilling downstream task has been implemented with 1K training samples where a connecting edge between two layers is tagged [MISSING], evaluated on 5K test cases.

The memorization evaluation has been implemented by computing the proportion of generated synthetic traces that exactly match training data in both call graph structure and all edge attributes, varying decoding temperature.
The distribution similarity evaluation using Earth Mover's Distance (EMD) has been implemented for in-degree, out-degree, and response time distributions on 10K synthetic traces.
The model size comparison experiment has been implemented by training Llama-3.2 1B, Llama-3.2 3B, Llama-2 7B, and Llama-2 13B under the recursive pre-training setup on the same dataset.
The ablation study removing intermediate instructions during instruction tuning has been implemented to measure the accuracy impact across varying temperatures.
The pre-training of TraceLLM (Llama-2 7B with LoRA) on 1.36M Alibaba v2022 call graph samples has been executed successfully without errors.
The instruction tuning of TraceLLM has been executed successfully for 4 epochs on 5% of training graphs reformatted with intermediate instructions.
The structured reasoning accuracy evaluation has been executed successfully for all three model variants (baseline, recursive, recursive+instruction) across 50 samples per (num_edges, depth) pair.
The distribution similarity experiment has been executed successfully, generating 50K synthetic call graphs and computing KL divergence and heavy-hitter accuracy for all methods.
The ML use case experiments (FIRM and TraceVAE) have been executed successfully with 5 random seeds, generating and filtering invalid synthetic traces before training.
The instruction-following evaluation has been executed successfully for high latency, uncommon communications, and their combination across 1K validation instructions.
All three downstream task fine-tuning experiments (uncommon communication prediction, missing attribute infilling, missing call infilling) have been executed successfully.
The model size comparison experiments (Llama 1B, 3B, 7B, 13B) have been executed successfully with the recursive pre-training setup.
The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 3a: the baseline model's accuracy drops below 25% for num_edges > 15, while the recursive model maintains approximately 30% higher accuracy.
The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 3b: the baseline model's accuracy approaches zero for depth > 4, while the recursive model maintains approximately 35% higher accuracy.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 3c: instruction tuning further improves accuracy by 23-36% over the recursive-only model, with the recursive+instruction model maintaining about 10% higher accuracy over recursive across all temperatures.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 4a: TraceLLM and GReaT achieve KL divergences of 0.16 and 0.11 respectively for popular call distributions, while the probabilistic model achieves 3.84 and TVAE achieves 0.74.
The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 4b: TraceLLM achieves over 90% heavy-hitter accuracy for K≤50 and 65% at K=500, outperforming the probabilistic model (59% at K=10, 23% at K=500).

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 5: SVMs trained on TraceLLM synthetic data achieve near-identical accuracy to those trained on real data (within 1.5 percentage points) for FIRM critical component extraction on apps A and B.
The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 5: TraceVAE models trained on TraceLLM synthetic traces achieve comparable ROC AUC to models trained on real data for anomaly detection.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 6: the instruction-tuned model successfully generates high-latency and uncommon-communication call graphs with higher accuracy than models without specific instructions, and can satisfy combined instructions despite not being trained on combined instruction samples.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 7 (left): the trace-pretrained model achieves 76.8% accuracy on uncommon communication prediction, outperforming original Llama-2 7B (60.6%) and Llama-3.1 405B in-context learning (45.6%).

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 7 (middle/right): the trace-pretrained model achieves over 70% accuracy on missing attribute infilling and 66% on missing call infilling, outperforming original Llama-2 7B by 30-40% and significantly outperforming Llama-3.1 405B.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 13: removing intermediate instructions during instruction tuning results in approximately 13% decrease in call graph generation accuracy across all sampling temperatures.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 14: larger models (13B) achieve significantly higher accuracy than smaller models (1B, 3B) especially at depth > 4, with a 20-percentage-point improvement over the 7B model.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 15: the baseline model memorizes 16-24% of training data, while recursive and recursive+instruction models show significantly lower memorization at 3-5%.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 16: TraceLLM achieves the lowest normalized EMD across in-degree, out-degree, and response time distributions, with 2.6x to 10x reduction compared to GReaT and the probabilistic model.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: Llama-2 7B fine-tuned on TraceLLM synthetic traces achieves 67.1% and 62.5% accuracy on high latency and uncommon communication prediction tasks respectively, close to the real-data trained model (68.3% and 65.3%).

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3: QwQ-32B with few-shot prompting achieves only 11-37% structured reasoning accuracy across depths 2-5, significantly below TraceLLM's performance (>75% at all depths).
